bootstrap.author: maplebound
bootstrap.title: What's the most underrated spot in your province?
bootstrap.body:
Everybody's got a place the tourism board forgot. Mine is the stretch of the Ottawa River up past Pembroke — paddled it last weekend and saw exactly two other boats and one extremely judgemental heron.

Sell me on yours. Bonus points if the parking is free and the chip stand takes cash only.

comments.author: codfather
comments.body: Bonavista, b'y. Everyone rushes off to Gros Morne and fair enough, but Bonavista's got puffins, icebergs in the right season, and a root cellar around every corner. You can stand where Cabot supposedly landed and there's a decent chance you'll have the view all to yourself.

comments.author: chinook_wind
comments.body: Writing-on-Stone, way down in the southeast corner of Alberta. Everyone does Banff and Drumheller, but the hoodoos along the Milk River at sunset are something else entirely. It was 34 degrees last time I went and I'd still go back tomorrow.

replies.author: maplebound
replies.body: Adding both of these to the list. The heron will be devastated to hear I'm seeing other rivers.

comments.author: regina_mona
comments.body: Grasslands National Park. It's a dark sky preserve, so once the sun goes down the Milky Way just sits there over the prairie like it's got nowhere better to be. You hear coyotes and your own heartbeat and that's about it.

replies.author: northof60
replies.body: If the Milky Way ever starts feeling crowded down there, come north. We keep the good sky in bulk.

comments.author: ferndalefog
comments.body: The Sunshine Coast. It's a 40 minute ferry from Vancouver and somehow still feels like a secret. Yes it rains there too. That's how we keep it ours.

comments.author: bluenoser_belle
comments.body: The Northumberland shore. The tourism folks claim it's the warmest salt water north of the Carolinas, and after one July swim at Melmerby I'm inclined to let them have it.

bootstrap.author: bluenoser_belle
bootstrap.title: Reminder that Halifax made the donair its official food, and the rest of you are still eating catch-up
bootstrap.body:
City council actually voted on it back in 2015. Democracy in action. Sweet garlicky sauce dripping down your wrist at 2 a.m. is a civic institution here, and I don't make the rules — I just enforce them.

What's your city's honest answer? Not your fancy food. Your 2 a.m. food.

comments.author: portage_and_main
comments.body: Winnipeg answers with the fat boy — a burger buried under chili and everything else the grill has on hand. It's not pretty and neither are we at 2 a.m.

comments.author: prairiedialup
comments.body: our late night food is a gas station hot dog. the gas station is 45 minutes away. builds character

comments.author: maplebound
comments.body: Ottawa runs on shawarma. There are corners downtown where you can see three shops from one spot, and every single one of them has somebody's undying loyalty.

replies.author: bluenoser_belle
replies.body: Shawarma is respectable and I'll allow it. But until there's sweet sauce involved, Halifax sleeps easy.

comments.author: codfather
comments.body: Come down George Street some night, my friend. By 2 a.m. it's chips, dressing and gravy or nothing at all. Stuffing on fries — we saw the future before the future did.

bootstrap.author: northof60
bootstrap.title: It's nearly midnight and I just mowed my lawn in broad daylight
bootstrap.body: The sun doesn't properly set here until around 11:30 this time of year, and even then it just sort of dips below the horizon and sulks. My neighbour waved at me like this is normal. It is normal. That's the part I can never explain to anyone down south.

comments.author: tundrabloom
comments.body: Meanwhile my zone 3 garden is drinking up every one of those long hours. The peas grow so fast in July you can practically hear them. Almost makes up for winter. Almost.

comments.author: prairiedialup
comments.body: mowing at midnight sounds nice until you remember the mosquitoes also get the extra hours

comments.author: stormglass
comments.body: Down here on the east coast the sun clocks out around 9 and the fog takes the night shift. I'd trade you for a week, just to see what my street looks like at 11 p.m.

replies.author: northof60
replies.body: Careful what you wish for. By August you start wanting the dark back. You won't get it, but you'll want it.

comments.author: regina_mona
comments.body: There's a stretch of a Saskatchewan July where the light at 10 p.m. turns everything gold and even the grain elevators look like they're posing. I get it.

bootstrap.author: portage_and_main
bootstrap.title: Timbit rankings. Sour cream glazed at the top. I will not be taking questions at this time.

comments.author: crokinole_king
comments.body: Correct top pick, which makes it worse that you left the rest unranked. Sour cream glazed, chocolate glazed, honey dip, old fashioned plain, then a large gap, then birthday cake, which is icing sugar cosplaying as a memory.

comments.author: chinook_wind
comments.body: Honey dip catching strays in here. It's the reliable friend of the box. Never the star, never lets you down.

replies.author: portage_and_main
replies.body: Honey dip is the beige minivan of Timbits, and I say that with love, because I drive a beige minivan.

comments.author: salt_beef_bucket
comments.body: All due respect to the box of wee doughnuts, but toutons with molasses beat any Timbit ever fried. Fight me, mainlanders.

comments.author: prairiedialup
comments.body: old fashioned plain. some of us were raised right

bootstrap.author: stormglass
bootstrap.title: The fog ate my whole street this morning and I had to take it on faith that the sidewalk continued
bootstrap.body:
Walked the dog at 6 and couldn't see the house across the road. By the time we reached the corner, the corner had opinions about whether it existed. The dog was unbothered. The dog is always unbothered.

By 10 it had burned off and everyone carried on like nothing happened. Maritime summers keep you humble.

comments.author: ferndalefog
comments.body: In Vancouver we just call that a personality. Though ours arrives in November and overstays until March, so I respect a fog with the decency to leave by mid-morning.

comments.author: codfather
comments.body: That's a light haze, my love. In St. John's the fog comes in so thick the seagulls file flight plans. One July it hung around so long I forgot what colour my own fence was.

replies.author: stormglass
replies.body: I knew someone from the Rock would show up to out-fog me. I accept my silver medal.

comments.author: bluenoser_belle
comments.body: The foghorn out by the harbour is honestly my white noise machine at this point. Visitors ask how I sleep through it. I ask how they sleep without it.

bootstrap.author: penaltyboxphil
bootstrap.title: You can tell you've landed in Canada by counting the hockey bags on the carousel in July
bootstrap.body: Flew home this week. Middle of summer. Four hockey bags came off the belt before my suitcase did. Nobody blinked. Somewhere, someone's kid has a tournament, because someone's kid always has a tournament.

comments.author: goalie_mom_deb
comments.body: One of those bags was probably ours. Summer tournament season is very real and my trunk hasn't been empty since 2019. The goalie pads ride shotgun at this point.

comments.author: portage_and_main
comments.body: The hockey bag is Canada's national luggage. Weddings, funerals, tournaments — one bag does it all, and it smells like all three.

comments.author: maplebound
comments.body: The real test is the Ottawa carousel in January. Hockey bags, hockey sticks, and one brave soul with golf clubs manifesting spring.

replies.author: penaltyboxphil
replies.body: Golf clubs in January is just a hockey player who got hurt. Trust me on that one.

drip.author: chinook_wind
drip.title: It hailed on my barbecue tonight and I just kept flipping burgers
drip.body: Blue sky at 5, golf balls at 6, blue sky again at 7. The burgers were fine. The lettuce in the garden has seen the end of days. Calgary summer is just weather roulette with better views.

comments.author: prairiedialup
comments.body: hail on the bbq is just nature seasoning your burgers with adrenaline

replies.author: chinook_wind
replies.body: adrenaline-seasoned is going on the menu, thank you

comments.author: tundrabloom
comments.body: My condolences to the lettuce. Every July I plant with hope and one eye on the sky. Zone 3 gardening is a long apology from the climate.

comments.author: bluenoser_belle
comments.body: It's the audacity of a sky that can't commit. Out east we at least get a warning — the fog clears its throat first.

drip.author: tundrabloom
drip.title: Zone 3 July report: the peas made it, the tomatoes are thinking about it
drip.body:
The peas came through like champions and the lettuce is bolting out of spite. The tomatoes are all vine and promises. Every year I tell them August is coming, and every year they act surprised.

Short season growers, how's your patch holding up?

comments.author: regina_mona
comments.body: The peas always come through on the prairies. Something in them understands the deal out here: grow fast, ask questions never.

comments.author: northof60
comments.body: Growing in buckets up here so I can chase the warm spots around the deck. The 20 hour days do half the work. The other half is me apologizing to a zucchini.

comments.author: maplebound
comments.body: Valley garden checking in: the zucchini has unionized. Please send recipes and possibly a mediator.

replies.author: tundrabloom
replies.body: There is no mediation with zucchini. Only surrender, and loaf after loaf of zucchini bread.

drip.author: codfather
drip.title: Screeched in two mainlanders this weekend and one of them kissed the cod twice for luck
drip.body:
Had some friends from Ontario down for the week. Did it proper — the recitation, the shot of screech, the cod. One fella was so nervous he kissed it twice, which as far as I'm concerned makes him extra Newfoundlander now.

They came for the icebergs and stayed for the kitchen party. That's usually the way of it.

comments.author: bluenoser_belle
comments.body: The east coast starter pack: come for the scenery, stay because someone handed you a spoon and now you're in the band.

comments.author: stormglass
comments.body: A kitchen party will do that. I've watched visitors show up as strangers at 8 and leave at 2 a.m. knowing all the words to songs they'd never heard before. The east coast doesn't convert people, it just reminds them.

comments.author: maplebound
comments.body: As an Ontarian I can confirm we practise saying yes b'y in the mirror for weeks before visiting. The cod kissing we leave to fate.

replies.author: codfather
replies.body: The mirror practise shows, my friend, but we appreciate the effort. Fate's usually had a few by then anyway.

drip.author: regina_mona
drip.title: The sky did that thing again last night
drip.body: Shelf cloud rolled in over the canola around 8, the whole horizon went green-grey, and then the light after the storm was so gold it looked staged. Living skies isn't a slogan, it's a daily programming schedule.

comments.author: prairiedialup
comments.body: saw it. the sky's been showing off all week. somebody should tell it we're already impressed

comments.author: chinook_wind
comments.body: That post-storm gold is the best light in the country and I'll argue it with anyone. It's the sky apologizing and overdoing it.

comments.author: northof60
comments.body: You folks get your drama in July, we get ours in the winter sky. Between us the country's covered year round.

drip.author: ferndalefog
drip.title: It hit 29 in Vancouver today and the entire city has filed a formal complaint
drip.body: No air conditioning, because why would we have that. Everyone's at the beach pretending to enjoy it while quietly wilting. The same people who spent February saying they'd take any sunshine at all. We contain no multitudes here, just a complaint for every season.

comments.author: drizzlecity
comments.body: Mancunian assessment: 29 with mountains and ocean access is not a crisis. That said, I've lived here long enough that I too was on the seawall complaining, so.

replies.author: ferndalefog
replies.body: The seawall complaining lane is the fastest moving lane in the city. Glad you found it.

comments.author: portage_and_main
comments.body: 29 in Winnipeg is a nice June morning. Come visit in August when it's 34 with humidity, we'll toughen you up. Or don't — the mosquitoes have a quota.

comments.author: maplebound
comments.body: Ottawa checking in at 31 with a humidex that should require a permit. You get an ocean breeze, we get a canal that just sits there judging us. No sympathy from the capital.

drip.author: crokinole_king
drip.title: Every cottage in this country has the same crokinole board with the same one missing disc
drip.body:
Spent the weekend at a buddy's place near Bancroft. Found the board in a closet under a Monopoly with no dice and a lighthouse puzzle, 998 pieces. The crokinole board was older than both of us and somebody had marked the 20 hole with nail polish. National heritage, honestly.

Played until 1 a.m. My flicking finger is still recovering.

comments.author: maplebound
comments.body: The nail polish 20 hole is a load-bearing detail. Every board has one repair like that — ours has a hockey tape patch on the rail from a dispute in 1997 that nobody will explain.

comments.author: stormglass
comments.body: My grandmother's board hung on the wall like art between games, and games were nightly. She'd knock your last disc off the board and tell you it was nothing personal. It was personal.

replies.author: crokinole_king
replies.body: Grandmothers are the apex predators of crokinole. Mine kept score in her head and never once lost track, or mercy.

comments.author: codfather
comments.body: We're a darts family ourselves but it's the same energy — one board, one missing dart, and an uncle whose throw everyone quietly clears a path for.

drip.author: maplebound
drip.title: Three days on the river, zero regrets, roughly four thousand mosquito bites
drip.body:
Did a stretch of the Madawaska with my brother. The portages were muddy, the tarp leaked exactly over my face, and a squirrel got into the trail mix on night two. Ten out of ten, already planning the next one.

There is no version of a canoe trip where everything goes right, and I'm fairly sure that's the actual point.

comments.author: tundrabloom
comments.body: The bites fade, the river stays. Also the squirrel earned that trail mix through sheer nerve. Respect.

replies.author: maplebound
replies.body: The squirrel had the confidence of an animal who has done this many times before. Earned every peanut.

comments.author: penaltyboxphil
comments.body: The mosquitoes are the home team out there. You're just visiting their barn.

comments.author: stormglass
comments.body: The tarp leaking directly over your face is the wilderness checking whether you're paying attention. Sounds like a proper trip to me.

late_comments.author: portage_and_main
late_comments.body: Late to this one but the Whiteshell, east of Winnipeg. Granite, clear lakes, and loons doing their whole sad beautiful thing at dusk. Manitoba keeps it quiet on purpose.

late_comments.author: chinook_wind
late_comments.body: Adding a late entry for Calgary: ginger beef. The story goes it was invented here at a Chinese restaurant decades back, and late night ginger beef has personally carried me through some winters.

late_comments.author: penaltyboxphil
late_comments.body: Played shinny outdoors up in Yellowknife once at 10 at night. Nobody turned the rink lights on. Nobody needed to. Best ice of my life.

late_comments.author: stormglass
late_comments.body: Arriving late to say chocolate glazed, and I say it as a peaceful person who has never once shared a 20 pack the way I promised to.

late_comments.author: northof60
late_comments.body: We don't get much summer fog up here, but ice fog in deep winter is its own animal. It doesn't roll in so much as the air just decides it's done being transparent.

late_comments.author: ferndalefog
late_comments.body: YVR in July is 60 percent hockey bags, 40 percent paddleboards, and one guy trying to convince the desk his bike box counts as a personal item.
